name: Intro to AI
goal: Final exam on 2026-05-01 — surface familiarity with all 13 topics in final_topics.md, confident on step-by-step computation

history.notes: Strong on slice-and-renormalize for conditionals, product rule, directionality (P(A|B) vs P(B|A)), and law of total probability multi-path form (Q4 stretch nailed). Initial pre-question: applied multiplication instead of division for conditional, but corrected after one teach-cycle. Q3 elaboration example (grass/soil) was conceptually right but lacked dramatic asymmetry — coached toward bird/wings-style examples. Stretch Q4 was interleaved with joint_from_prior; correct.

concepts.name: Linear Algebra for ML (dot products, matrix mul)

history.notes: Diagnostic: knew g and h definitions; imprecise on admissibility (said h < actual cost, should be ≤)

FAIL — same diagnostic bug 15 days later: still says h must be 'less than' true cost, missed h(S)=5 as admissible. Strict-vs-non-strict inequality has not healed. Re-coached with mnemonic 'admissible = never overestimates' and the perfect-heuristic edge case (h=h* is the BEST kind of heuristic, would be banned under strict-<). Retest tomorrow with the same question.

Retest passed. h*(S)=7 problem: correctly identified h(S)=5 ≤ 7 and h(S)=7 ≤ 7 as both admissible (equality case is what he missed yesterday). h(S)=8 implied not admissible by exclusion. Coaching from yesterday stuck.

Practice exam Q1: admissibility correct, but flipped which heuristic is "better" (said lower wins; correct: HIGHER admissible h is more informed). D1 traversal: optimal path correct (S-A-C-G cost 6), but g(n) computed as last-edge-cost instead of cumulative-from-start. Drill the cumulative-g concept on exam.
Diagnostic: clearly explained minimax vs expectimax distinction (chance nodes, expected value)

Re-touch after fuzzy interleave on 2026-04-28 mdps session. Computed deck's expectimax example tree cleanly (C1=8, C2=4, C3=7, ROOT=8). Vocabulary recovery: yesterday said T was 'above chance nodes', today correctly placed T as 'leaf weighting' (edges from chance node down). Mapping is locked. Slight notational sloppiness — dropped the 0 leaf from C3 sum, but harmless. Mastered.

Pre-question: spontaneously reached for conditional probability to describe transitions — good evidence prior session stuck. Strong on 5-tuple definition once introduced; correctly identified T values, summed to 1 (interleaved-style sanity check from probability_basics, passed). Q1 discounted utility (4-step reward seq with negatives): clean. Q3 interleaved expectimax: partial — got the structure (max/chance/T mapping) but vocabulary was imprecise ('max probability of higher reward' instead of 'max expected utility'; said T sits 'above' chance nodes when it labels edges below). Q4 stretch (deriving T from empirical counts) was missed — couldn't recover the form N(s,a,s')/N(s,a) — but called the gap out himself. Policy-vs-plan distinction explained well in own words ('waterfall plan vs state-reactive').
Practice exam Q3: computational parts (T, R, sum-to-1) all correct. Skipped (d): MDP-vs-Markov-chain distinction. Lock answer: MDPs have actions (agent choices); Markov chains do not.

FAIL by rubric, but signal is mixed. During teaching: clean execution of V_1(Warm) and V_2(Warm) — formula structure is internalized. Assessment under time pressure: V_2(Cool) wrong (Q1 and Q2 share same bug). Specific error: when Fast action from Cool branches stochastically to Cool (50%) AND Warm (50%), used V_1(Warm)=1 in BOTH terms instead of V_1(Cool)=2 in the Cool-branch and V_1(Warm)=1 in the Warm-branch. Discount placement (γ next to V_k(s')) was correct in Q2. Ran out of time on Q3 (convergence) and Q4 (policy extraction). NEXT SESSION: 5-min retest with a Q1-style problem to verify the tracking bug is fixed; then move on. Don't re-teach the structure — it's there.

Retest after yesterday's fail. Computed V_3(Cool) = 5.0 with V_2 = [3.5, 2.5, 0]. Q_3(Cool, Fast) correctly applied V_2(Cool)=3.5 to the Cool-stay branch and V_2(Warm)=2.5 to the Warm-jump branch — yesterday's tracking bug fixed. Self-annotated 'Two possible outcome states -> Cool, Warm' showing deliberate attention to the bug-prone step.

Practice exam: BUG REGRESSED on Q10. V_2(S1) used V_1(S1)=3 when destination was S2 (needed V_1(S2)=5.5). Same 4/28 tracking bug. RE-DRILLED on D2 (states A/B): V_2(A) computed correctly using V_1(A)=1.4 and V_1(B)=5 in the right branches. Drill worked, but bug is fragile under non-trivial setups. EXAM-DAY VIGILANCE: when stochastic action branches to multiple destinations, label each V explicitly with its destination state.

Taught from scratch. Pre-question revealed shaky LoTP application (couldn't independently set up P(X_2) from P(X_1) and transition); unblocked by reframing as 'same shape as factory-machines LoTP from 4/27'. Algebraic shortcut for stationary distribution clicked: Q1 (fresh chain A/B with self-loops 0.6/0.8) computed pi_A=1/3, pi_B=2/3 cleanly. Q2 verification of friend's (0.5, 0.5) claim: applied transition correctly to get (0.4, 0.6) — concluded not-stationary correctly, but compared to wrong reference number (true stationary value 0.67 instead of candidate 0.5). Q3 elaboration: correctly named LoTP — important recovery from this morning's joint_from_prior fail where joint/marginal labels got scrambled. Q4 stretch on 3-state generalization: didn't grasp 'linear independence' of balance equations — coached on n-1 + normalization. Source attribution corrected: stationary distribution material is in lecture 11 (Bayes Net), not lecture 5 (MDP I).
Practice Q9: pi_X=1/3, pi_Y=2/3 cleanly with balance + normalization.
Diagnostic: applied P(A,B)=P(A)P(B) (independence) incorrectly; confused P(B|¬A) with P(A|B); does not know law of total probability by name

Partial: applied LoTP machinery correctly (got 0.38) but labeled the result as joint P(rain, traffic) when it's actually marginal P(traffic). Joint vs marginal vocabulary still slippery. Joint is single product-rule term P(t|r)*P(r) = 0.24; marginal is sum across both branches via LoTP = 0.38. Note: yesterday's mdps Q4 had him correctly distinguishing T(s,a,s') as conditional from joint counts — so the conceptual machinery is there, but joint-vs-marginal labels get scrambled under exam-style framing. Retest tomorrow.

Retest passed cleanly. Computed joint P(rain, traffic) = P(t|r)*P(r) = 0.36 (one product-rule term) and marginal P(traffic) = 0.54 (LoTP across both rain branches). Labels stayed straight — yesterday's joint/marginal confusion is resolved.
Practice Q7: joint=0.18, marginal=0.215 (rounded 0.22), Bayes ≈0.82 — clean.

Quick definitional pass. Correctly classified clustering, regression-from-labeled-history, and anomaly detection without labels. Source attribution corrected to lecture 13 (where the supervised vs unsupervised slide actually lives).
Practice Q4: 4/4 correct.

Captured the 'more data, more perspectives → less variance on new observations' angle (correct outcome). Cleaner mechanism worth holding: augmentation prevents the model from memorizing specific pixel patterns, forcing it to learn invariant concepts → reduces overfitting. Source attribution corrected from lecture 13 to lecture 16 (where the actual augmentation slides live).
Practice Q5: cleanly stated "learn underlying concept instead of memorizing pixel formations".

FAIL on a careless-reading mistake, not a conceptual gap. Treated training_set_size and mini_batch_size as the same number (50), missing that the question gave training_set=5000, batch=50. Should have computed 100 updates/epoch, 2000 over 20 epochs. Conceptual understanding of 'epoch = full pass' was correct. Coaching: read questions twice on the actual exam. Source corrected to lecture 16.
Retest with fresh numbers (8000 set, batch 80, 15 epochs). Computed 100 updates/epoch and 1500 total cleanly. Read the numbers carefully this time. Coaching stuck.
Practice Q6: 80 updates/epoch, 640 total, noise ranking SGD>mini>full all correct.
Diagnostic: correctly computed 3x3 output for 5x5 input, 3x3 filter, stride 1. Did not attempt stride-2 bonus

FAIL — dropped the +1 in formula on stride-2 case. Wrote (7-3)/2 = 2 → 2x2 output. Correct: floor((input-filter)/stride) + 1 = 3 → 3x3 output. The +1 WAS present in his stride-1 diagnostic, so he knows it; just failed to apply. Re-anchored with fence-post intuition (number of fits = number of gaps + 1).

Retest passed. 9x9 input, 3x3 filter, stride 2 → (9-3)/2 + 1 = 4 → 4x4. Asked a thoughtful clarifying question about why floor() encapsulates the numerator (correct answer: needed when stride doesn't divide the gap evenly, e.g., 8x8 input case).
Practice Q8: 24x24, 15x15, 3x3 — all three with floor handled. Mastered.

concepts.name: MLP and Logistic Regression Full Computation
sources: sources/intro_to_ai/12 Machine Learning Perceptrons and Logistic Regression (1).pdf

history.notes: Taught LR forward pass, multi-class softmax (interleave with ce_loss — clean), MLP with hidden layer + ReLU. Q1 LR: dot product + bias + sigmoid saturation reasoning correct (z=-2, output near 0). Q2 multi-class softmax: clean computation with (0.67, 0.24, 0.09), sanity-checked sum to 1. Q3 MLP: mechanics (dot product, ReLU, bias, sigmoid) all correct, but flipped the W matrix interpretation — used 'columns=output' convention (standard X@W) instead of the deck's 'row i = weights into hidden node i' convention. Got h_1=0 instead of 2.5, h_2=1.5 instead of 0. Also slipped notation conflating z (pre-activation) with h (post-ReLU). Coached: follow the professor's notation exactly on exam. Q4 stretch: nailed the universal-approximation insight — composition of linear functions is linear, depth needs nonlinearity. Asked good clarifying questions during teaching: 'isn't activation supposed to be sigmoid(z)?' and 'why is weight_bias = -1?' — showing he was actively reconciling terminology.
Practice Q11: LR z=0.5 correct, MLP h=0 (ReLU saturated), z_out=0.5 correct.

concepts.name: Computing Cross-Entropy Loss
sources: sources/intro_to_ai/12 Machine Learning Perceptrons and Logistic Regression (1).pdf

history.notes: Diagnostic: did not know CE formula — needs L = -log(p_true) taught from scratch

Taught from scratch with motivation (catastrophic-confidence intuition before formula). Correctly computed both multi-class CE (Q1: -log_2(0.15)) and binary CE (Q2: t=0 branch). Q3 elaboration was solid on softmax + CE direction but conflated softmax normalization with conditional-probability normalization from prior session, and stopped short of differentiability/gradient as the why-training-works mechanism. Q4 stretch (training-loop generalization): structure correct, notation sloppy with class-index/one-hot conflation. Pre-question revealed he didn't initially know what 'catastrophically wrong' meant; clarifying that example was the unlock.
Practice Q12: both losses (0.322 and 1) correct, comparison and intuition right.

Pre-exam morning crash session. Taught GAN minimax objective, the non-saturating G-loss conversion (min log(1-D(G(z))) -> max log(D(G(z)))), and the CE-loss equivalence for D. Q1 Ds targets: clean. Q2 saturation reasoning: captured the right idea informally. Q3 named the equivalence (cross-entropy) cleanly. Surface familiarity achieved - 13 of 13 exam topics now covered.
